GCC Economic Integration — Where Do We Stand?

Executive Summary | Gulf Observatory for Economic Integration Paths

GCC-Stat

April 2026

What This Index Measures

GCC-Stat has developed the GCC Economic Integration Index (GCCEII) to track how deeply integrated the six GCC economies are with each other. The index combines data on trade flows, financial linkages, labour mobility, physical connectivity, economic diversification, and macroeconomic alignment into a single score from 0 to 100, where higher scores reflect deeper integration.

The index covers 2015 to 2024 and is built using internationally recognised composite indicator methodology (COINr, developed by the EU Joint Research Centre). This summary presents the main findings for the GCC as a whole.

How Has GCC Integration Evolved?

The GCC’s overall integration score has remained relatively flat throughout the entire period, dipping to its lowest point in 2020 during the COVID-19 pandemic before gradually recovering.

The GCC integration score stood at 45.4 in 2015 and reached 47.4 in 2024 — a modest improvement of about 2 points over a decade. The score dipped to a low of 40.3 in 2020 before gradually recovering. Despite high-profile integration initiatives, the pace of progress has been slow.

The relatively flat trend reflects two structural realities. First, Saudi Arabia and the UAE together account for over three-quarters of GCC output, so the composite is heavily shaped by their performance — and both have structural features (large oil-export sectors, limited intra-GCC trade) that hold the aggregate back. Second, while individual policy areas have improved, no single dimension has advanced enough to shift the overall score decisively upward.

Which Areas Drive Integration — And Which Hold It Back?

The overall score is built from six dimensions. The chart below shows how each dimension scored in 2024.

In 2024, Convergence (macroeconomic alignment) is the highest-scoring dimension at 59.5, reflecting the GCC’s shared currency peg and coordinated economic policies. Trade Integration is the weakest area at 32.5, reflecting the fact that most GCC countries still export primarily to markets outside the region.

The table below summarises each dimension’s performance and direction of travel over the decade:

Dimension 2024 Score 10-Year Trend Key Message
Trade 32.5 ⟶ Flat Chronically the weakest area. Intra-GCC trade remains limited for most member states.
Financial 53.5 ↑ Gradual rise Steady, quiet improvement in banking linkages and capital market integration.
Labor 34.6 ⟶ Flat Labour mobility across GCC borders remains underdeveloped despite large expatriate workforces.
Infrastructure 57.6 ↑ Recovery Collapsed in 2020 (COVID) but has fully recovered. The strongest recent improvement.
Sustainability 58.4 ~ Volatile Sensitive to oil price cycles. Improved but fluctuates with hydrocarbon revenue changes.
Convergence 59.5 ⟶ Stable The strongest dimension. Shared currency peg and coordinated policies keep this high.

Country Differences at a Glance

Member states vary considerably in their integration levels. The table below shows the 2024 overall score for each country.

Country Score Main Strength Main Challenge
Bahrain 64.3 Financial integration and trade openness Small size limits scale; labour scores moderate
UAE 53.6 High infrastructure scores, strong convergence Trade intensity low; labour market constrained
Qatar 51.7 High labour mobility, financial integration Trade and sustainability scores weak
Oman 46.8 Recovering trade and infrastructure post-2021 Trade still low; sustainability underdeveloped
Kuwait 45.5 Large infrastructure investment, diversification Trade intensity lowest in GCC; labour restricted
Saudi Arabia 44.2 Financial depth and macroeconomic convergence Labour mobility moderate; infrastructure variable

Bahrain leads the GCC with the highest integration score (64.3), driven by its open financial sector and trade linkages. Saudi Arabia saw a significant decline from 2016 to 2021 before recovering — its trajectory matters most for the GCC aggregate given its economic size. Qatar’s recovery since the 2021 Al-Ula reconciliation is the most notable improvement in recent years.

Three Priority Recommendations

1. Accelerate intra-GCC trade diversification

Trade is the weakest integration dimension and has barely moved in a decade. Most GCC exports still go to markets outside the region. Strengthening intra-GCC value chains in non-oil sectors — manufacturing, food, technology services — and removing the remaining regulatory barriers to cross-border trade would have the highest impact on the overall index. This requires both national-level economic diversification and GCC-wide facilitation frameworks.

2. Develop a unified GCC labour mobility framework

Despite the Gulf’s reliance on tens of millions of cross-border workers, labour mobility between member states remains structurally limited by separate regulatory frameworks. A unified GCC work permit, mutual recognition of professional qualifications, and harmonised visa rules would directly improve one of the index’s most stagnant dimensions — and deliver real economic benefits to workers, employers, and member state economies.

3. Support Saudi Arabia’s integration deepening

Saudi Arabia accounts for 42% of GCC economic output, meaning its integration score is the single largest determinant of the GCC composite. Saudi Arabia saw a significant decline in its integration scores from 2016 to 2021 before recovering in recent years. Targeted bilateral initiatives — particularly deeper Saudi-UAE financial market linkages and expanded Saudi participation in intra-GCC trade — would have a proportionally large impact on the regional aggregate and signal real momentum in the integration agenda.

Prepared by GCC-Stat | Gulf Observatory for Economic Integration Paths
Data source: GCCEII composite indicator pipeline, output/gcceii_scores.csv
